trigger.reason: Meta announced a new 'AI Mode' feature on Facebook with cross-platform integration, a product release from a major tech company that plausibly triggers sympathy-fade selling in adjacent sectors (customer-service software, edtech, low-code tools).

primary_event.headline: Meta's new 'AI Mode' on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
primary_event.summary: Meta announced a rollout of new AI features on Facebook that integrate across its platform ecosystem. The feature pulls from public information to enhance user engagement, representing Meta's latest effort to compete in the AI race.

secondary_events.headline: Salesforce acquires AI customer service platform Fin for $3.6B

discovery.trigger_acknowledgment: Analyzed Meta's 'AI Mode' Facebook feature rollout as a capability demo that triggered sympathy-fade selling in AI-adjacent sectors including customer-service software, edtech, low-code tools, and marketing automation.
discovery.run_summary: The Meta AI Mode announcement is a consumer-engagement feature with no obvious direct enterprise-software displacement vector, yet sympathy fades hit edtech (LRN crushed) and vertical-workflow SaaS (NCNO, TYL). Most movers in the basket actually rose (semis, edge-AI, machine vision), reflecting AI-beneficiary positioning rather than panic. The cleanest unjustified panic cases are LRN, NCNO, and TYL — names with regulatory/contract moats that a Facebook AI feature cannot plausibly disrupt.

discoveries.name: Stride, Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: 10-K Risk Factors center on per-pupil funding formulas, charter renewals, ESSA accountability, state regulatory frameworks, and accredited transcripts. AI is mentioned only as a peripheral 'keep pace with technology' risk, not as a competitive threat to the accredited K-12 school-as-a-service model.
discoveries.thesis: Stride's revenue is contractually tied to per-pupil state funding for accredited virtual public schools — parents enroll children for diplomas, transcripts, and state-funded education, none of which a Meta Facebook AI feature can deliver. The sympathy-fade premise ('AI tutors replace edtech') is the wrong frame; Stride is a regulated school operator, not a tutoring app. The move appears to be an extreme overreaction conflating Stride with consumer edtech apps.
discoveries.what_confirms: Continued strong enrollment data in next quarterly update; no charter non-renewals; state per-pupil funding stability.
discoveries.what_kills: Evidence of state legislatures cutting virtual charter funding, charter non-renewals, or material enrollment declines in the next data release; a concurrent negative catalyst we missed.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Accredited K-12 operators get lumped with consumer edtech apps on every AI headline despite running on state funding formulas and charter contracts — the regulatory/accreditation moat is structural, not technological.
discoveries.catalyst: Meta's new 'AI Mode' on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: LRN is on the edtech sympathy-fade list every time a major AI lab ships consumer features, despite no business-model overlap with consumer AI products.
research_pointers: Check most recent monthly enrollment disclosure and trend vs. prior year
Verify no concurrent 8-K (charter loss, guidance cut, officer departure)
Look at FY2026 state per-pupil funding trends in Stride's top states (CA, OH, PA)
Cross-check whether the magnitude of today's move suggests a non-public catalyst

discoveries.name: nCino, Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: 10-K Risk Factors explicitly discuss AI as both a competitive risk and core growth strategy — nCino has invested in AI capabilities and integrated them into its banking workflow platform. The core moat remains deep integration into regulated FI workflows (multi-year deployments, examiner-grade audit trails), which a Meta consumer AI feature does not threaten.
discoveries.thesis: nCino sells loan-origination workflow software to regulated banks where examination compliance, multi-year deployments, and integration with core banking systems form structural moats. A Meta consumer AI Mode for Facebook has zero plausible blast radius into bank loan-origination software. The negative move is pure thematic SaaS-AI sympathy.
discoveries.what_confirms: Stable subscription renewal rates in next quarter; continued FI customer wins; no large bank customer churn.
discoveries.what_kills: Evidence that a major bank customer is replacing nCino with an in-house AI/LLM-based workflow; deterioration in subscription bookings; renewal rate decline.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Vertical workflow SaaS in regulated industries (banking, government, healthcare) gets sympathy-faded with consumer SaaS despite the regulatory examination moat that consumer AI fundamentally cannot cross.
discoveries.catalyst: Meta's new 'AI Mode' on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: NCNO falls into the 'AI eats SaaS' thematic sympathy basket on any major lab consumer release, despite vertical-workflow banking software having minimal exposure to consumer AI features.
research_pointers: Check next quarter renewal rate and net retention metrics
Monitor for large FI customer wins or losses
Track adoption of nCino's own AI features (Banking Advisor, etc.) as a leading indicator

discoveries.name: Tyler Technologies, Inc.
discoveries.filings_evidence: 10-K Risk Factors emphasize public-sector procurement cycles, long sales cycles, fixed-price contracts, and the unique challenges of selling to governments. AI is discussed primarily as a capability Tyler is incorporating into its products and as a cybersecurity threat vector — not as an existential competitor. No language identifies generative AI products as a competitive displacement risk to court records, ERP, or municipal software.
discoveries.thesis: Tyler sells court records, municipal ERP, and government workflow systems with multi-year procurement cycles, RFP-based sales, and decades-long client tenure. A Meta consumer AI feature has zero realistic path to displacing system-of-record software for courts and cities — no municipal CIO replaces a case management system with an LLM. The selloff is pure SaaS-AI thematic sympathy.
discoveries.what_confirms: Continued bookings growth in SaaS subscriptions; stable government RFP wins; recurring revenue stability.
discoveries.what_kills: Government RFPs explicitly favoring AI-native competitors over Tyler; large municipal customer non-renewals; concurrent guidance cut.
discoveries.what_to_learn: Government-vertical SaaS has the strongest procurement-based moat of any software vertical — sympathy fades on consumer AI releases are mechanically wrong here, and these names tend to recover quickly.
discoveries.catalyst: Meta's new 'AI Mode' on Facebook pulls from public info across its platforms
discoveries.catalyst_evidence: TYL is grouped with SaaS names on any AI consumer-feature headline despite serving a sector (government) with the slowest possible procurement and the highest switching cost.
research_pointers: Check SaaS bookings growth in next quarter
Monitor whether Tyler's own AI rollouts (in-product) gain customer traction

skipped.reason: Moved up, not down — no panic to fade
Moved up, not down — sympathy beneficiary, not victim
Moved up — direct AI capex beneficiary already extended on 5-day
Moved up — semi-cap beneficiary already extended on 5-day
